trials.title: Effects of LRYGB on Pharmacokinetics of Nine CYP Probe Drugs
trials.brief_summary:
Due to anatomical and physiological changes caused by the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (RYGB) bariatric surgery, drug bioavailability after the surgery can be altered. Therefore, post-operative dose adjustment in these patients can be required.

The aim of the study was to investigate the effects of laparoscopic Roux-en-Y gastric bypass (LRYGB) surgery on drug pharmacokinetics and cytochrome P450 (CYP) mediated metabolism using a cocktail of nine CYP probe drugs.

The cocktail covers nine main CYP enzymes: melatonin (CYP1A2), nicotine (CYP2A6), bupropion (CYP2B6), repaglinide (CYP2C8), losartan (CYP2C9), omeprazole (CYP2C19/CYP3A4), dextromethorphan (CYP2D6), chlorzoxazone (CYP2E1), midazolam (CYP3A4).

The changes in pharmacokinetic parameters of the drugs as well as modulation of the activity of CYPs are evaluated before and one year after LRYGB. In the study, the patients administering drug cocktail before surgery and 1 year after LRYGB are served as their own controls.

trials.title: EMI-EHP Weight Management and Type 2 Diabetes Pragmatic Trial
trials.brief_summary:
This is a pragmatic, 24 month, single-center, randomized, open-label, parallel-group trial comparing an obesity-centric approach with a medically-supervised and comprehensive weight loss program (Cleveland Clinic's Endocrinology and Metabolism Institute's Integrated Weight Management Program) augmented by AOMs, vs. an obesity-centric approach with a medically-supervised and comprehensive weight loss program without AOMs, vs. the current usual care approach to general health management.

Informed consent will be obtained. IRB approval of the study will be obtained. 300 subjects (employees or spouses covered by our EHP) will be randomized 1:1:1 to receive either an obesity-centric approach with AOM therapy (N=100), an obesity-centric approach without AOM therapy (N=100), or the current usual care approach to general health management (N=100).
trials.why_stopped: After an unplanned yet IRB approved interim analysis, it was found that there was not a significant difference between the 3 arms of the study. The study was closed due to futility. All Patients are off study and this study will not resume.

clinical_trials.summary:
**Bupropion in Obesity — Analysis Summary**

**FDA Approval Status:** This exact indication does not appear on bupropion's labels in this run. However, bupropion is a key component of the fixed-dose combination product naltrexone/bupropion (Contrave®/Mysimba®), which is FDA-approved for chronic weight management — a closely related but distinct regulatory entity. The supervisor should reconcile whether the combination approval constitutes coverage for this query.

**Overall Trial Activity:** There are 54 trials on record for bupropion in obesity, spanning Phase 1 through Phase 4 and Not Applicable (real-world/observational designs). The portfolio is dominated by the naltrexone SR/bupropion SR combination, with bupropion rarely studied as a standalone agent in this indication.

**Completed Trials:** 29 trials are recorded as completed. Among these, 6 are on record as completed Phase 3 and 6 as Phase 2/Phase 3. The pivotal Phase 3 program for naltrexone/bupropion includes:
- **NCT00532779** ("COR-I"): Safety and efficacy of two doses of naltrexone SR/bupropion SR in overweight/obese subjects — published readout available (PMID 20673995).
- **NCT00567255** ("COR-II"): Safety and efficacy in overweight/obese subjects — published readout available (PMID 23408728).
- **NCT00456521** ("COR-BMOD"): Naltrexone SR/bupropion SR with intensive behavior modification — published readout available (PMID 20559296).
- **NCT00474630** ("COR-Diabetes"): Naltrexone SR/bupropion SR in obese subjects with Type 2 diabetes — published readout available (PMID 24144653).
- **NCT01764386**: Method-of-use study assessing effect on body weight and cardiovascular risk factors — published readout available (PMID 28026920).
These completed Phase 3 trials with published readouts form the evidentiary basis for the combination's regulatory approval. A large real-world cardiovascular safety study (NCT06090461, Not Applicable phase) also completed with a published readout (PMID 40104005).

**Terminated Trials:** 4 trials were terminated. The most notable is **NCT01601704** ("The LIGHT Study"), a large Phase 3 cardiovascular outcomes study of naltrexone SR/bupropion SR in overweight/obese subjects with CV risk factors — it was terminated and has a published reference (PMID 26954408); the why_stopped text was not filed on CT.gov, but the LIGHT Study is publicly known to have been stopped for regulatory/business reasons related to interim data access disputes rather than a safety or efficacy signal. A Phase 4 trial (NCT04531176) in Type 2 diabetes and obesity was stopped for futility after an IRB-approved interim analysis found no significant difference between arms. A second Phase 4 cardiovascular outcomes study (NCT02638129) was also terminated with no why_stopped text filed. A Phase 2 trial in schizophrenia/obesity (NCT03132571) was stopped due to funding loss — a neutral business reason.

**Competitive Landscape:** The obesity space is highly competitive, with multiple agents active across phases. Naltrexone/bupropion competes against GLP-1 receptor agonists (notably semaglutide/Wegovy, which appears in the landscape and even in a head-to-head comparison trial NCT05579249), as well as other approved and investigational weight-management therapies.

**Repurposing Assessment:** Bupropion as a standalone agent for obesity has limited dedicated trial evidence; its obesity-related development has been conducted almost exclusively as the naltrexone/bupropion combination. The combination has a well-characterized Phase 3 program with multiple published pivotal trials and is commercially available. There are no safety or efficacy stopping signals on record for bupropion alone in obesity. The primary repurposing question — whether bupropion monotherapy warrants further development in obesity — remains largely unexplored in the trial record, while the combination product occupies the regulatory and commercial space.

blurb.prose: Bupropion is approved for obesity only as the fixed-dose combination product naltrexone/bupropion (Contrave/Mysimba); bupropion monotherapy is not indicated for obesity and the obesity trial record is dominated by the combination regimen.

summary:
Ranked repurposing signals for bupropion:

1. attention deficit hyperactivity disorder — moderate literature (Cochrane review of 6 RCTs, meta-analysis of 5 RCTs, systematic review); Phase 3 completed (NCT00048360), no regulatory progression
2. cocaine dependence — moderate mixed literature (RCTs showing benefit only with contingency management co-intervention); Phase 3 completed (NCT01077024), no regulatory progression, no active programs
3. fibromyalgia — weak literature (narrative review, benefit for comorbid depression only, not pain); Phase 2/Phase 3 completed (NCT04747314, fibromyalgia as secondary mesh term, Phase 2-scale), no dedicated program

Demoted — approval relationship:
- obesity (approved only as combination product with naltrexone; bupropion monotherapy not indicated)
